county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: El Paso County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Colorado Springs. Named for the pass (Ute Pass). Founded by General William Palmer (1871). Military installations (Fort Carson, USAF Academy, Peterson/NORAD). Health resort heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: El Paso County Clerk and Recorder in Colorado Springs holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Colorado Springs was founded in 1871 by General William Jackson Palmer as a resort city. The Pikes Peak Regional History Center at the Penrose Library in Colorado Springs is an excellent genealogical resource with city directories, photograph collections, newspaper indexes, and manuscript collections. Garden of the Gods, Manitou Springs, and the Pikes Peak region attracted health seekers (tuberculosis patients) in the late 1800s -- sanatorium records may document families. Fort Carson (established 1942) military records are at NARA. The US Air Force Academy (1954) and NORAD/Peterson Air Force Base records also document military families. Cripple Creek mining district was in El Paso County until Teller County was created in 1899. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for El Paso County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: El Paso County Deed Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: El Paso County Clerk and Recorder, Colorado Springs
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books, mortgages, and liens for El Paso County from 1861; County Clerk and Recorder maintains original records and indexes

source_profiles.source_name: El Paso County Marriage Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: El Paso County Clerk and Recorder, Colorado Springs
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and certificates for El Paso County from 1861; Colorado required marriage licenses from territorial period; state vital records registration began 1907

source_profiles.repository: El Paso County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for El Paso County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for El Paso County including homestead entries and mineral patents; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Elbert County formed 1874 from Douglas County and extinct Greenwood County. County seat is Kiowa. Named for Territorial Governor Samuel Elbert. Eastern plains ranching and farming. Greenwood County (extinct 1874) merged into Elbert.
county_metadata.research_tips: Elbert County Clerk and Recorder in Kiowa holds records from 1874 (created from Douglas County and extinct Greenwood County). A ranching and farming county on the eastern Colorado plains. Elbert was one of the counties affected by the open-range cattle era of the 1870s-1880s. The now-extinct Greenwood County (1870-1874) was merged into Elbert -- check Colorado State Archives for any surviving Greenwood County records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential for tracking pioneer settlers. The Elbert County Museum has local history materials. Church records from Kiowa, Elizabeth, and Simla document rural families. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The county's proximity to Denver means some families appear in both county and Denver records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Elbert County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Fremont County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Canon City. Named for explorer John C. Fremont. Colorado State Penitentiary (1871). Royal Gorge. Florence oil field (one of earliest in US). Coal mining.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fremont County Clerk and Recorder in Canon City holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Canon City is home to the Colorado Territorial (later State) Penitentiary, established 1871 -- inmate records at the Colorado State Archives document prisoners from across the state. The Royal Gorge area attracted railroad rivalry (Denver & Rio Grande vs. Santa Fe Railroad, 1879). Canon City and Florence were oil production centers (Florence oil field, 1862, was one of the earliest in the US). Coal mining in the Chandler, Coal Creek, and Rockvale areas drew immigrant workers. The Canon City Public Library has local history collections. Fremont County Pioneer Association records. Colorado State Archives holds county records on microfilm. Check original territorial records for 1861-1870s era.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Fremont County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garfield County formed 1883 from Summit County. County seat is Glenwood Springs. Named for President Garfield. Resort and railroad town. Coal mining heritage. Doc Holliday buried in Glenwood Springs (1887).
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County Clerk and Recorder in Glenwood Springs holds records from 1883 (created from Summit County). Glenwood Springs was a resort town and railroad hub (Denver & Rio Grande). Doc Holliday died in Glenwood Springs (1887) -- local cemetery and historical records. Coal mining at Carbondale, New Castle, and the Coal Basin drew Italian, Austrian, and Slavic immigrant workers. The 1893 coal mine explosion at Vulcan Mine and other mining disasters are documented in state mining reports. Garfield County Libraries have regional history collections. Glenwood Springs Historical Society. Check Summit County for pre-1883 records. BLM GLO records for land patents and mining claims. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Garfield County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Gilpin County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Central City. Heart of the 1859 gold rush ('richest square mile on earth'). Central City, Black Hawk. One of Colorado's smallest counties by area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gilpin County Clerk and Recorder in Central City holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Central City and Black Hawk were at the heart of the 1859 Colorado gold rush ('the richest square mile on earth'). Gilpin County has exceptionally rich mining-era records: mining district minutes, claim records, stock certificates, and mine reports. The Gilpin History Museum in Central City has mining-era collections. The Teller House and Central City Opera House are landmarks with associated records. Many ethnic communities worked the mines: Cornish (experienced hard-rock miners), Irish, Welsh, German, Italian, and Chinese. The Gilpin County Observer newspaper has been published since 1868. Colorado School of Mines archives in Golden have mining industry records. Colorado State Archives holds county records on microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Gilpin County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grand County formed 1874 from Summit County. County seat is Hot Sulphur Springs. Named for the Grand River (now Colorado River). Mountain ranching and tourism. Grand Lake. Moffat Road railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grand County Clerk and Recorder in Hot Sulphur Springs holds records from 1874 (created from Summit County). A mountain county with ranching, timber, and limited mining heritage. Hot Sulphur Springs was the county seat and an early resort. Grand Lake was a tourist destination from the 1870s. The county was on the route of the Moffat Road (Denver Northwestern & Pacific Railway). Grand County Historical Association museum has local collections. Kawuneeche Valley has Ute history. Check Summit County for pre-1874 records. BLM GLO records document homesteads. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records in the scattered communities are sparse but document pioneer families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Grand County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Gunnison County formed 1877 from Lake County. County seat is Gunnison. Major mining heritage (Crested Butte, Gothic, Irwin, Tin Cup). Western State University. Parent county to Delta, Montrose, Ouray, Pitkin, San Miguel, and Hinsdale.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gunnison County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1877 (created from Lake County). Major silver and gold mining county with famous camps including Crested Butte, Gothic, Irwin, and Tin Cup. Crested Butte was a coal mining town with Croatian, Italian, and Slavic immigrant workers. The Gunnison County Pioneer and Historical Society museum has mining-era collections. Western Colorado University (formerly Western State) in Gunnison has regional archives. The county encompassed a huge area before Delta, Montrose, San Miguel, Ouray, Pitkin, and Hinsdale were carved from it. Check Lake County for pre-1877 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO records document mining claims and land patents. Church records from various denominations serve the mining communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Gunnison County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hinsdale County formed 1874 from Conejos and Lake counties. County seat is Lake City. One of the least populated counties in Colorado and the US. Silver mining. Alferd Packer cannibalism case (1874).
county_metadata.research_tips: Hinsdale County Clerk and Recorder in Lake City holds records from 1874 (created from Conejos and Lake counties). One of the least populated counties in Colorado, Hinsdale was a silver mining area with Lake City as its only significant town. The infamous Alferd Packer cannibalism case (1874) is part of the county's history and documented in court records. Hinsdale County Museum in Lake City has local collections. Lake City's Silver World newspaper (from 1875) documents the mining era. The county's small population means most families are traceable through a limited set of records. Check Conejos and Lake counties for pre-1874 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims and patents.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Hinsdale County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Huerfano County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Walsenburg. Named for the Huerfano Butte. Deep Hispanic heritage. Major coal mining county (CF&I). Italian, Slavic, Greek, and Japanese immigrant communities. Colorado Coalfield War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Huerfano County Clerk and Recorder in Walsenburg holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. The county has deep Hispanic heritage predating the territory. Coal mining dominated the economy from the 1870s through the mid-1900s -- the Colorado Fuel & Iron Company (CF&I) operated major mines. The Ludlow Massacre (1914) occurred just south in Las Animas County, but Huerfano County miners were deeply involved in the Colorado Coalfield War. Italian, Slavic, Greek, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrant coal miners are documented in CF&I company records (held at Steelworks Center of the West in Pueblo and the Bessemer Historical Society). Catholic parish records in Walsenburg serve the Hispanic and Italian communities. Spanish Peaks Library District has local history. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Huerfano County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) for Huerfano County Hispanic communities; many records predate civil registration; some early records are in Spanish; Archdiocese of Denver and (for pre-territorial records) Archdiocese of Santa Fe hold parish registers

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jackson County formed 1909 from Larimer and Grand counties. County seat is Walden. Named for President Andrew Jackson. North Park mountain valley. Ranching. One of Colorado's least populated counties. Never reached by railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County Clerk and Recorder in Walden holds records from 1909 (created from Larimer and Grand counties). North Park is a high mountain valley with ranching heritage. One of Colorado's least populated counties. The county was accessed via stagecoach and later by road -- no railroad ever reached Walden. Check Larimer and Grand counties for pre-1909 records. North Park Pioneer Museum in Walden has local collections. Church records in Walden (Catholic, Protestant) document the small community. BLM GLO records document homesteads and ranch land patents. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The North Park area has Ute and Arapaho history. Hunting and outfitting were important industries -- outfitter records may document seasonal workers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Jackson County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Jackson County Deed Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: Jackson County Clerk and Recorder, Walden
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books, mortgages, and liens for Jackson County from 1909; County Clerk and Recorder maintains original records and indexes

source_profiles.source_name: Jackson County Marriage Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: Jackson County Clerk and Recorder, Walden
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and certificates for Jackson County from 1909; Colorado required marriage licenses from territorial period; state vital records registration began 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Jackson County Probate Records -- District Court / Colorado State Archives
source_profiles.repository: Jackson County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for Jackson County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.source_name: Jackson County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO Records
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Jackson County including homestead entries and mineral patents; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Jackson County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Jackson County from 1890; county formed after 1885 state census -- check parent county for earlier enumerations
